data.title: A fix for fixed income lies with the government or RBI?
data.description: The bond market has two big events to take cues from in February
data.author: MoneyControl

data.title: Forthcoming changes in acquisition financing rules set to give debt market a fillip
data.description:
India’s international finance centre can also help anchor global capital

India is opening a new chapter in deal finance. For the first time, Indian companies can contemplate borrowing in foreign currency to fund share purchases through the external commercial borrowing route. At the same time, the Reserve Bank of India has set the stage for Indian banks to finance acquisitions by Indian corporates. Together, these moves close a long-standing gap that pushed Indian acquisition finance deals overseas. They also promise a more practical and market-led regime at home.

Hitherto choices were limited

Until now, the choices were narrow and slow. Listed debentures held by foreign portfolio investors and non-bank funds were possible. But the process was heavy and took time. Disclosure demands were high. Electronic book building added friction. Only the most determined borrowers used this path.

The result was predictable. Debt moved to foreign hubs like Singapore. Structures grew complex and lender's risk rose. Deal design often followed financing limits rather than business logic.

A two-track approach

Related Stories

  • 'President Trump doesn’t impact my investing cycle': Raamdeo Agrawal explains why
  • Budget 2026: Will markets see a pre-Budget rally? Don't expect broad-based buying, say analysts
  • OPINION | India’s civilisational journey is complicated; neat demarcations are ahistorical

The new approach moves on two tracks. Draft changes to the foreign exchange rules make acquisition finance a permitted end use for external commercial borrowing. This lets Indian buyers use foreign currency loans for mergers and share purchases.

In parallel, the central bank has announced a framework that will allow Indian banks to lend for acquisitions. This gives borrowers a choice between foreign currency and rupee debt. It also brings speed and a local syndication path.

Key changes make the foreign borrowing route workable.

Acquisition finance is now a clear end use. The borrower pool is wider. All Indian entities formed under law qualify, including real estate and infrastructure trusts. Borrowing limits scale with financial strength. Ceilings can reach one billion dollars or three times net worth. Regulated entities may get more room. The minimum average maturity is a uniform three years. Pricing is liberal. Cost caps are gone. Rates must match prevailing market conditions, including any penalties. Lenders can now price the real risks in each deal. There is also more flexibility to on-lend ECB within groups. That supports holding company and operating company structures, and platform strategies.

Pricing freedom and tenor are the game changers. Acquisition loans carry varied risks. Lenders need room to adjust at the start and through covenants across the life of the loan. A three-year minimum maturity helps build repayment structures that make sense. Market linked pricing allows true risk-based underwriting. This brings India closer to global practice.

These shifts should bring more work onshore. Documentation and diligence can sit in India. Structured solutions for stressed assets can fit better within flexible pricing and longer tenors. The regime moves from a paper permission to a practical, bankable reality.

Competitive edge 

Borrowers will gain vast choice. The bias toward offshore hubs should ease. Sectors that need large cheques, such as infrastructure and real estate, should benefit from market-linked pricing and lighter compliance compared to listed debentures. Over time, as authorised dealer banks set benchmarks, pricing and structures should converge toward global norms for stronger credits. India’s international finance centre can also help anchor global capital with local standards on pricing, documents and risk.

What will be the impact on private credit funds and domestic AIFs, who so far they had a golden period with double digit returns and downside protection in the form of hybrid / convertible instruments? Will they need to come to the drawing board given the stiff competition they will face from domestic and foreign banks? Also, it remains to be seen how domestic banks play this out – they will need to be nimble and agile.

There are open questions. Will the same rules apply to listed and unlisted targets? How will trusts on-lend or take security under sector rules? What evidence will banks accept to show that pricing reflects market conditions? How will penalties be set? Clarity will be needed in the final rules and in day-to-day bank practice.

The direction is clear. India is moving from rigid protection to market confidence. If the final rules are pragmatic and banks set smart policies, more deal debt will remain in India. Lenders will broaden. Execution will be faster and cleaner. India’s acquisition finance market can deepen and mature in line with global norms.

(Shubhangi Garg is Partner, Shardul Amarchand Mangaldas & Co.)

(Views are personal and do not represent the stand of this publication.)

data.title: NLC India to develop energy renewable projects in Gujarat; share price in focus
data.description:
NLC India

NLC

  • Watchlist
  • Portfolio
  • Message
  • Follow
  • Day High
  • Day Low
  • Volume (NSE)
More
×
Add to Watchlist

NLC India's share price will remain in focus on January 14 after the company signed a pact with the Gujarat government for the development of renewable energy projects.

“…has signed a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) with the Government of Gujarat during the Vibrant Gujarat Regional Conference for the development of large-scale renewable energy projects in the state, including solar, wind, hybrid and battery energy storage projects, with an aggregate investment potential of approximately ₹25,000 crore,” the company said in its exchange filing.

The proposed projects will be developed through the company’s wholly owned subsidiary, NLC India Renewables (NIRL).

Catch all the market action on our live blog

The company board in its meeting held on January 12 gave in-principle approval for the listing of NLC India Renewables Ltd (NIRL) through dilution of up to 25 percent equity stake, in one or more tranches via a public offer, subject to necessary approvals from competent authorities and approved an investment of up to Rs 66.60 crore in NIRL.

Related Stories

  • Stocks to Watch Today: Kotak Mahindra Bank, ICICI Lombard, Tata Elxsi, Interarch Building, NLC India...
  • Royal Alpha Growth, Vorton Capital among 4 investors acquire over 6% stake in Gabion Technologies on...

Additionally, the board has approved the declaration of an interim dividend of 36 percent, amounting to Rs 3.60 per equity share of face value Rs 10 for the financial year 2025–26.

In the previous trading session, the share closed at Rs 255.20, down Rs 0.65, or 0.25 percent.

The share touched a 52-week high of Rs 292.35 and a 52-week low of Rs 185.85 on 07 October, 2025 and 17 February, 2025, respectively.

Currently, the stock is trading 12.71 percent below its 52-week high and 37.32 percent above its 52-week low.

The market capitalisation of the company stands at Rs 35,386.97 crore.

Just Dial share price will remain in focus on January 14 following the company reported a mixed performance in the quarter ended December 2025 (Q3FY26).

The company's Q3 net profit declined 10.2% year-on-year to Rs 117.9 crore compared with Rs 131.3 crore in the same period last year.

However, revenue grew 6.4% YoY to Rs 305.6 crore from Rs 287.3 crore.

The earnings before interest, tax, depreciation and amortization (EBITDA) increased 9.6% YoY to Rs 95.2 crore versus Rs 86.8 crore last year and margin expanded to 31.1% from 30.2%, YoY.

Catch all the market action on our live blog

In the previous trading session, the share closed at Rs 735.40, up Rs 13.90, or 1.93 percent.

The share touched a 52-week high of Rs 1,049.85 and a 52-week low of Rs 700.00 on 21 April, 2025 and 07 April, 2025, respectively.

Currently, the stock is trading 29.95 percent below its 52-week high and 5.06 percent above its 52-week low.

he market capitalisation of the company stands at Rs 6,254.18 crore.

data.title: First Tick: Top global cues to watch in today’s trade
data.description:
Market Today

Indian benchmark indices, Sensex and Nifty, are likely to see a muted start on January 14, with cues coming from GIFT Nifty, which was trading with little change at around 25,760.50.

Track the latest updates on GIFT Nifty right here on Moneycontrol.

In another volatile session, the Indian equity market ended lower on January 13, with the Nifty slipping below 25,750 amid selling pressure in auto, consumer durables, pharma and realty stocks.

At close, the Sensex was down 250.48 points or 0.30 percent at 83,627.69, and the Nifty was down 57.95 points or 0.22 percent at 25,732.30.

Here is how financial markets across the globe fared overnight:

GIFT Nifty (Flat)

Related Stories

  • Daily Voice | Union Budget must pivot from recovery-led to productivity-led growth: Qode’s Gaurav ...
  • Trading Plan: Can Nifty 50 defend 25,600, Bank Nifty climb above 59,800 amid rangebound trade?
  • Trade setup for January 14: Top 15 things to know before the opening bell

The GIFT Nifty is trading flat at around 25,760.50, indicating a muted opening for the day.

Asian Equities (Up)

Asian shares posted a modest gain at the open, while Japanese equities extended their record-breaking run fueled by a weaker yen.

US Equities (Down)

US ​stocks ended lower on Tuesday, led by a drop in financial shares as comments from JPMorgan executives ‌added to worries about US President Donald Trump's recent proposal for a cap on credit‑card rates.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 398.21 points, ‍or 0.80%, to 49,191.99, the S&P 500 lost 13.53 points, or 0.19%, to 6,963.74 and the Nasdaq Composite lost 24.03 points, or 0.10%, ‌to ‌23,709.87.

US Bond Yield (Flat)

The yield on 10-year Treasuries and 2-year Treasuries was trading with little changed at 4.18% and 3.53%, respectively.

Dollar Index (Gains)

The US dollar recovered ground to near a one-month high in early Asian trade on Wednesday after US CPI data that was broadly in line with estimates, firming up expectations that the Federal Reserve will remain on hold later this month despite unprecedented pressure from the White House to lower interest rates.

Asian Currencies (Down)

All the Asian currencies were trading in the red in the early trade on Wednesday, with the Japanese Yen leading the losers, followed by Indonesian Rupiah, Philippine Peso, China Renminbi, Taiwan Dollar, Malaysian Ringgit.

Crude (Slips)

Brent crude notched its biggest four-day gain since June as Trump ramped up rhetoric on Iran.

Silver (Up)

Gold rose toward a record high as weaker-than expected US inflation data supported the case for more interest rate cuts, while the geopolitical situation remained combustible.

Fund Flow Action

On January 13, the Foreign Institutional Investors (FIIs) sold equities worth around Rs 1499 crore, while Domestic Institutional Investors (DIIs) purchased equities worth Rs 1181 crore.

Hope you're all set for today's trade. We wish you a profitable day ahead.

The benchmark indices gave up some of the previous day’s gains, with the Nifty 50 declining 0.22 percent on January 13. Market breadth was moderately weak, with about 1,491 shares declining against 1,370 advancing shares on the NSE. The market may see consolidation as long as it trades below the midline of the Bollinger Bands. Below are some short-term trading ideas to consider:

Amol Athawale, VP Technical Research at Kotak Securities

TVS Motor Company | CMP: Rs 3,755.4

After declining from higher levels, TVS Motor rebounded from its retracement zone and witnessed a robust recovery. Additionally, on the intraday charts, the stock has given a breakout from its sloping trendline. The upward move in the stock suggests a new leg of a bullish trend from current levels.

Related Stories

  • Trade setup for January 14: Top 15 things to know before the opening bell
  • Royal Alpha Growth, Vorton Capital among 4 investors acquire over 6% stake in Gabion Technologies on...

For the next few trading sessions, Rs 3,620 could be the trend-decider level for bulls. If the stock sustains above this level, a further uptrend towards Rs 4,010 can be expected.

Strategy: Buy

Target: Rs 4,010

Stop-Loss: Rs 3,620

Jindal Steel | CMP: Rs 1,010.8

After a remarkable up move on the daily chart, Jindal Steel witnessed profit booking at higher levels. Following the recent sell-off, the downward momentum in the stock has stalled. Moreover, the stock has found support near its demand zone.

This formation suggests a revival of the uptrend from current levels in the near future. For positional traders, Rs 980 would be the decisive level. If the stock trades above this level, the uptrend is likely to continue towards Rs 1,080. However, if it closes below Rs 980, traders may prefer to exit long positions.

Strategy: Buy

Target: Rs 1,080

Stop-Loss: Rs 980

Union Bank of India | CMP: Rs 166.19

Following its strong up move and a brief consolidation over the last few sessions, Union Bank of India is gaining further traction for a fresh up move. The chart structure indicates a bullish continuation pattern, which is likely to persist in the near term.

As long as the stock trades above Rs 161, the bullish bias is likely to continue, with an upside target of Rs 178.

Strategy: Buy

Target: Rs 178

Stop-Loss: Rs 161

Ashish Kyal, CMT, Founder and CEO at Waves Strategy Advisors

Dredging Corporation of India | CMP: Rs 1,105

On the daily chart, Dredging Corporation of India has been trading in a broad consolidation range of Rs 892–1,071 over the past few weeks, indicating an accumulation phase. In the previous session, the stock finally gave a range breakout and managed to close above the range at Rs 1,105. This move was supported by a strong rise in volumes, adding credibility to the breakout.

Additionally, the MACD has witnessed a bullish crossover for the first time since December 2025, which is a positive sign. For now, any dips towards Rs 1,070–1,075 can be considered a buying opportunity for an upside move towards Rs 1,150. On the downside, the Rs 1,020 level is expected to act as a crucial support.

Strategy: Buy

Target: Rs 1,150

Stop-Loss: Rs 1,020

Tech Mahindra | CMP: 1,614.8

On the daily chart, Tech Mahindra has been moving within a larger triangle pattern since the beginning of December 2024 and is now trading close to the trendline resistance. At the start of this week, the stock found support at the 50-day EMA (Exponential Moving Average) and witnessed a sharp rebound, forming higher highs and higher lows, which signals an improving price structure.

For now, a break above Rs 1,635 can confirm a breakout from the triangle pattern, potentially resulting in a fresh rally towards Rs 1,750–1,800, as long as the EMA support near Rs 1,558 remains protected on the downside.

Strategy: Buy

Target: Rs 1,750, Rs 1,800

Stop-Loss: Rs 1,558

ICICI Bank | CMP: Rs 1,437

At the start of the month, ICICI Bank confirmed a breakout from a double-bottom pattern and has successfully retested the breakout zone. The stock is now trading near its prior swing high of Rs 1,442, indicating sustained strength. Over the past two sessions, prices have consistently protected the previous day’s low on a closing basis, which is a positive sign.

Additionally, the Know Sure Thing (KST) indicator has already given a bullish crossover and is now trading above the zero line, suggesting that positive momentum is likely to continue. A decisive break above the Rs 1,442 level is required for bullish momentum to extend, which could push prices higher towards Rs 1,540, while Rs 1,390 is expected to act as the nearest support.

Strategy: Buy

Target: Rs 1,540

Stop-Loss: Rs 1,390

data.title: Trading Plan: Can Nifty 50 defend 25,600, Bank Nifty climb above 59,800 amid rangebound trade?
data.description:
Nifty Trading Plan for January 14
  • Nifty 50 expected to remain in a 25,450–26,000 range in short term
  • If index breaks below 25,600, a fall toward 25,450 is possible
  • Convincing move above 25,900 can open the door to 26,000 zone
Did our AI summary help?

The Nifty 50 is expected to remain in a 25,450–26,000 range in the short term, as a decisive break on either side of the range can provide a firm directional move. If the index breaks below 25,600 (100-day EMA), a fall toward 25,450 is possible. However, a convincing move above 25,900 (50-day EMA) can open the door to the psychological 26,000 zone. Meanwhile, the Bank Nifty needs to give a strong close above 59,800 for a move toward 60,000–60,100. However, sustaining below this level can keep the banking index in consolidation mode, with support at 59,300–59,200, experts said.

On January 13, the Nifty 50 fell 58 points (0.22 percent) to 25,732, while the Bank Nifty advanced 128 points (0.22 percent) to 59,579. Market breadth was weak, as a total of 1,491 shares saw selling pressure compared to 1,370 shares that gained on the NSE.

Nifty Outlook and Strategy

Ashish Kyal, CMT, Founder and CEO at Waves Strategy Advisors

At the start of the week, Nifty staged a smart recovery from the 25,500 mark due to rising hopes of a trade deal between India and the US. However, in the previous session, the index failed to extend the move and remained range-bound with high volatility amid the weekly F&O expiry. Nifty successfully defended the 25,500 support and is now consolidating near this level.

Related Stories

  • Trade Spotlight: How should you trade TVS Motor, Union Bank of India, Dredging Corporation, Tech Mah...
  • Trade setup for January 14: Top 15 things to know before the opening bell

Amid the earnings season, IT-related stocks closed higher after moderate updates from IT majors, helping Nifty limit further downside. Meanwhile, key global events, such as the Supreme Court verdict on Donald Trump’s tariffs and rising US–Iran tensions, continue to cap upside momentum. With multiple triggers lined up, this week remains crucial. The next two sessions of price action are likely to set the tone for the rest of the month.

Unless we see a break of the range in either direction, stock-specific action is expected due to the ongoing earnings season. For Nifty, a break below 25,470 levels can accelerate further selling pressure, while a break above 25,940 is required to revive buying interest for a move toward 26,070 or higher.

Key Resistance: 26,070

Key Support: 25,470

Strategy: Long positions can be created if Nifty moves toward 25,690 and breaks above 25,760, with a stop-loss at 25,680 and a target of 25,900.

Preeti K Chabra, Founder of Trade Delta

On the daily timeframe, Nifty formed a bearish candle and continues to trade within a steep downward-sloping channel, where rebounds are met with fresh selling pressure—clearly reinforcing bearish control. The index is trading below the 40-day EMA at 25,937 and the 20-day SMA (middle Bollinger Band) at 26,006, indicating sustained selling pressure at higher levels and a lack of meaningful follow-through on pullback rallies.

On the downside, 25,544 emerges as a crucial support level, coinciding with the 78.6% Fibonacci retracement of the rally from the November 7 low of 25,318 to the January 5 high of 26,373. A decisive breakdown below this level could accelerate further weakness. The daily RSI at 41.1, positioned below its signal line, reflects strong bearish momentum and highlights the absence of buying strength.

On the weekly timeframe, Nifty is currently taking support near the 20-week SMA (middle Bollinger Band) at 25,645. A sustained breach below this crucial support would likely resume medium-term selling pressure and deepen the corrective phase.

From a derivatives perspective, weekly options data continues to indicate a neutral undertone, suggesting the absence of aggressive directional positioning at present.

Overall, the broader structure remains bearish, and unless Nifty decisively reclaims its key moving averages, the index is likely to remain under pressure with a downside bias.

Key Resistance: 25,937, 26,006

Key Support: 25,645, 25,544

Strategy: Consider selling Nifty Futures near 25,937 for a target of 25,645, with a stop-loss at 26,006.

Bank Nifty - Outlook and Positioning

Ashish Kyal, CMT, Founder and CEO at Waves Strategy Advisors

Bank Nifty has found support near the 58,800 level for the fifth time and has now seen a sharp rebound of more than 850 points over a span of just two days. In this rebound, PSU banks were the major contributors compared to private banks, highlighting their outperformance.

This rebound has pushed prices to retrace 50% of the prior fall that started at the beginning of this month. The next target is the 61.8% retracement, which comes near the 59,850 level.

In the previous session, Bank Nifty formed a hammer-like daily candlestick pattern, which is a reversal candlestick pattern. For confirmation, a close above the prior day’s high of 59,767 is required. If this happens, it can result in deeper pullbacks.

Bank Nifty has continued to consolidate within a broader range of 60,000 to 58,800 levels for almost three months. For now, a break above 59,850 can trigger a fresh round of buying toward 60,060 levels. A break below 59,300 can result in an attempt to test the major price action support area of 58,800 levels.

Key Resistance: 60,060

Key Support: 58,800

Strategy: Long positions can be created above 59,850, with a stop-loss at 59,650 and targets of 60,060 followed by 60,200 levels.

Preeti K Chabra, Founder of Trade Delta

Bank Nifty is trading above the 20-day SMA (middle Bollinger Band) at 59,420 and the 40-day EMA at 59,141, reflecting an improving price structure and strengthening near-term sentiment.

The index is also holding above the 38.2% Fibonacci retracement level at 59,184, calculated from the rally between the November 7 low of 57,157 and the January 5 high of 60,437, thereby reinforcing a strong near-term support base. On the upside, 59,800 is acting as an immediate resistance, and a sustained breakout above this zone could open the door for further upside momentum.

Momentum indicators remain supportive of the positive bias. The daily RSI at 53.5, though currently trading below its signal line, is trending upward, indicating a gradual build-up in bullish momentum.

From a derivatives perspective, monthly options data remains constructive, as unwinding in in-the-money Call options suggests a bullish outlook.

Overall, Bank Nifty is showing signs of recovery, and traders may consider adopting a buy-on-dips strategy as long as the index holds above the immediate support zone.

Key Resistance: 59,800, 60,100

Key Support: 59,420, 59,184

Strategy: Buy Bank Nifty Futures near the cash reference level of 59,420 for an upside target of 59,800 followed by 60,100, while maintaining a stop-loss at 59,184.

data.title: Daily Voice | Union Budget must pivot from recovery-led to productivity-led growth: Qode’s Gaurav Didwania
data.description:
Gaurav Didwania is the Partner and Fund Manager at Qode Advisors PMS
  • Union Budget needs a decisive shift from recovery-led to productivity-led growth
  • Expect a strong focus on semiconductor policy in the Budget
  • With inflation broadly under control but global uncertainties still elevated, aggressive rate cuts by RBI appear unlikely in 2026
Did our AI summary help?

“If I were framing the Union Budget 2026, my agenda would be to shift decisively from recovery-led growth to productivity-led growth,” said Gaurav Didwania, Partner and Fund Manager at Qode Advisors PMS, in an interview with Moneycontrol.

He believes India has already demonstrated fiscal credibility, and that the next leap must come from improved efficiency and capital formation rather than headline spending.

Didwania also expects a strong focus on semiconductor policy in the Budget, though he feels the emphasis is likely to shift from fresh announcements to execution discipline.

According to him, with inflation broadly under control but global uncertainties still elevated, aggressive rate cuts by the Reserve Bank of India appear unlikely in 2026.

What would be your agenda for the Union Budget 2026 if you were the Finance Minister?

If I were framing the Union Budget 2026, my agenda would be to shift decisively from recovery-led growth to productivity-led growth. India has already demonstrated fiscal credibility, so the next leap must come from efficiency and capital formation rather than headline spending.

First, I would prioritise private capex crowding-in by extending targeted tax incentives for manufacturing, logistics, and clean energy, while rationalising non-merit subsidies. India’s capex-to-GDP ratio has improved to around 4.1 percent, but to sustain growth above 7 percent, it needs to move closer to 5 percent over the next three years.

Second, I would focus on urban infrastructure and housing, especially in Tier-2 and Tier-3 cities, where job creation multipliers are higher. Labour-intensive sectors like textiles, food processing, and electronics assembly should receive outcome-linked incentives rather than blanket schemes.

Finally, I would invest heavily in human capital through AI skilling, vocational training, and higher education reform, because India’s demographic dividend will only pay off if productivity rises. Budget 2026 should be less about populism and more about building long-term economic resilience.

Do you expect a strong focus on semiconductor policy in the Budget?

Yes, but the emphasis will likely shift from announcements to execution discipline. India has already committed over Rs 76,000 crore under the semiconductor and display ecosystem policy. Budget 2026 should focus on ensuring that this capital translates into viable fabs, OSAT facilities, and a functioning domestic supply chain.

The key challenge is not funding alone but time-to-market, yield efficiency, and talent availability. Semiconductor fabs are capital intensive with long gestation periods, and global experience shows that success depends on ecosystem depth rather than standalone plants.

I expect the Budget to focus more on design-linked incentives, chip packaging, testing, and compound semiconductors, where India has comparative advantages. Encouraging global design houses and start-ups can create high-value intellectual property even before full-scale manufacturing matures.

The real success metric will not be the number of MoUs signed, but whether India can reduce strategic dependence in sectors such as defence, telecom, and electric vehicles over the next decade. Budget 2026 should therefore be pragmatic rather than aspirational.

Do you expect the current quarterly earnings season to be strong?

The current earnings season is likely to be selectively strong rather than broad-based. Sectors linked to domestic demand, including banking, capital goods, defence, and infrastructure, should report steady growth supported by credit expansion of around 14 to 15 percent and continued government spending. However, export-oriented sectors such as IT services, chemicals, and some discretionary consumption segments may see muted growth and margin pressure.

Global growth remains uneven, and higher wage costs and pricing pressure are still visible in IT. Corporate balance sheets are healthier than in previous cycles, and interest coverage ratios have improved materially, which provides resilience. That said, revenue growth rather than cost control will be the key differentiator this season.

The takeaway for investors is that this earnings cycle will reward stock selection over sectoral bets. Companies with pricing power, strong order books, and clean balance sheets are likely to outperform, while leveraged or globally exposed firms may lag. This is a constructive but not euphoric earnings environment.

Do you believe 2026 will emerge as the year of AI? What could be the biggest risk to Indian IT companies from AI?

2026 could mark the transition of AI from experimentation to enterprise-scale deployment, but it is likely to be evolutionary rather than explosive. AI adoption will be uneven across industries and driven more by return on investment than hype.

For Indian IT companies, the biggest risk is not AI itself but the speed at which clients internalise productivity gains. Generative AI can compress traditional billing models by automating repetitive tasks, reducing headcount-intensive work that historically drove revenues. The winners will be firms that monetise outcomes instead of effort, offering AI-led platforms, industry-specific solutions, and intellectual property-based pricing.

The laggards will be those relying on legacy time-and-material models. At the same time, AI is a massive opportunity. Indian IT has scale, data engineering capability, and deep domain knowledge. The key will be reskilling, as companies that fail to redeploy talent risk margin erosion. AI will reshape the industry rather than replace it, but adaptation speed will determine winners.

Are you bullish on the copper sector, considering the rising demand?

Structurally, yes. Copper sits at the centre of the energy transition, electrification, and urbanisation themes. Electric vehicles use nearly four times more copper than internal combustion engine vehicles, and renewable energy grids are highly copper-intensive. Global demand is expected to grow at around 3 to 4 percent CAGR over this decade.

On the supply side, constraints are emerging. New copper mines face long approval timelines, declining ore grades, and geopolitical risks, which could keep supply tight and support prices over the medium term. That said, copper remains a cyclical commodity and is sensitive to China’s growth trajectory and global manufacturing trends.

Short-term volatility should be expected, especially if global growth slows or financial conditions tighten. Investors should approach copper with a long-term structural lens rather than short-term price momentum. Companies with low-cost reserves, strong balance sheets, and downstream integration are best positioned to benefit from this multi-year theme.

Do you expect the economy to grow beyond the estimated 7.4 percent in FY26?

India has the potential to exceed 7.4 percent growth in FY26, but this will depend on three variables: private capex, rural demand, and global stability. Public capex alone cannot sustain higher growth indefinitely. If private investment accelerates, supported by high capacity utilisation and improving corporate profitability, growth could surprise on the upside. Early signs are encouraging, particularly in manufacturing, defence, and renewable energy.

However, external risks remain. Slower global trade, geopolitical disruptions, or commodity price shocks could cap upside. A recovery in rural consumption will also be critical, as it has lagged urban demand. India is transitioning from a cyclical rebound to a structural growth phase, but sustaining 8 percent growth will require reforms in land, labour, and education.

FY26 could be strong, but consistency matters more than a single year of outperformance.

Do you foresee further rate cuts by the RBI during the current year to boost growth?

The RBI’s approach is likely to remain cautious and data-dependent rather than growth-driven. With inflation broadly under control but global uncertainties still elevated, aggressive rate cuts appear unlikely. If inflation sustains near the 4 percent target and global central banks ease meaningfully, the RBI may have room for modest, calibrated cuts of around 25 to 50 basis points.

However, the central bank will continue to prioritise financial stability and currency management. India’s growth challenges are not primarily due to high interest rates but stem from structural and demand-side factors. Rate cuts alone cannot substitute for reforms or a revival in private capex.

The bigger support to growth will come from liquidity management, efficient policy transmission, and coordination between fiscal and monetary policy rather than headline rate cuts. Monetary policy will play a supportive role rather than a heroic one.

Disclaimer: The views and investment tips expressed by experts on Moneycontrol are their own and not those of the website or its management. Moneycontrol advises users to check with certified experts before taking any investment decisions.
data.author: MoneyControl

data.title: Asian shares post small gain, Yen weakens past 159
data.description:
Attention in Asia will once again be on Japan, where Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s reported plan for a snap election fueled a rally in stocks while pushing down bonds and driving the yen deeper into the intervention-risk zone.
  • Japanese stocks rise as yen weakens, Nikkei 225 up 0.9% on snap election reports
  • US S&P 500 falls from record high after JPMorgan's investment-banking fees miss
  • December US core CPI rises 0.2%, annual rate at 2.6%, matching a four-year low
Did our AI summary help?

Asian shares posted a modest gain at the open, while Japanese equities extended their record-breaking run fueled by a weaker yen.

The Nikkei 225 Stock Average advanced 0.9%, with the Japanese currency slipping past the 159-per-dollar level to the weakest since July 2024. Stocks in the country have jumped and the currency fallen amid reports of a snap election. Shares in South Korea edged up, having posted a gain on every trading day of 2026.

In Tuesday’s US session, the S&P 500 fell from an all-time high, as JPMorgan Chase & Co. led a slide in lenders after its investment-banking fees missed guidance. US benchmarks dropped after December inflation data did little to dent expectations that the Federal Reserve will pause interest-rate cuts.

Traders are going into Wednesday awaiting a possible US Supreme Court ruling on President Donald Trump’s global tariffs that were announced in April.

Meanwhile, the cooler-than-expected US consumer price index reading reinforced bond traders’ expectations that the Fed will wait until mid-year to cut rates. Even after Fed Chair Jerome Powell and his board lowered the benchmark three times last year, money markets have continued to project the next reduction only in mid-2026.

“The initial excitement sparked by a cooler-than-anticipated core CPI was short-lived,” said Jose Torres at Interactive Brokers. “The reversal was influenced, in part, by the report’s failure to pull forward the next expected rate reduction from June to April, as fixed-income watchers project Powell’s December cut will be his last at the helm.”

Following JPMorgan’s results Tuesday, earnings from megabank rivals Bank of America Corp., Wells Fargo & Co., Citigroup Inc., Goldman Sachs Group Inc. and Morgan Stanley are slated for Wednesday and Thursday. The group is expected to post its second-highest annual profit ever, boosted by Trump’s policy changes.

Traders also are mindful of the potential for a US Supreme Court ruling Wednesday on tariffs the White House has been enforcing. An adverse ruling could draw a negative market reaction, even as the administration has alternative legal avenues for most of the levies.

Elsewhere, Brent crude notched its biggest four-day gain since June as Trump ramped up rhetoric on Iran, while silver also extended its recent rally to cap its best three-day streak on record.

Attention in Asia will once again be on Japan, where Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s reported plan for a snap election fueled a rally in stocks while pushing down bonds and driving the yen deeper into the intervention-risk zone. Japanese five-year yields rose to 1.615%, the highest since the tenor’s 2000 debut.

Success at the polls for Takaichi, who ascended to the premiership in October, would provide a mandate for her to continue hawkish diplomacy and pro-stimulus policies.

Back to the US inflation data, the December core CPI, excluding the often volatile food and energy categories, increased 0.2% from November. On an annual basis, it advanced 2.6%, matching a four-year low.

The reading is perhaps a more convincing sign that inflation is on a downward path, since a number of caveats in November’s report contributed to a significant pullback in the annual core CPI.

“Given the quirks of November’s dual-month report, it’s surprising not to see more numerous large month-over-month readjustments,” said Stephen Kates at Bankrate.

“Consumers can breathe a sigh of relief that we didn’t snap back to the 3% annual inflation rate. Although today’s reading doesn’t demonstrate additional progress for inflation, it doesn’t take a step backwards either.”
data.author: MoneyControl
